origins: early kumaon & the katyuris कूर्मांचल — कत्यूरी युग 
## in two lines

kumaon is *kurmanchal* — the land where vishnu, as the tortoise kurma, is said to have stood on a hill near champawat. its first great dynasty, the katyuris, left behind the temple cities of jageshwar, baijnath and katarmal — and kings so vivid they are still summoned by name in jagar rituals today.

## what it is

**the name.** "kumaon" derives from *kurmanchal / kurmachala* — the hill (kurmashila, near today's champawat) where vishnu's tortoise avatar stood; for centuries the name applied only to that small tract ("kali kumaon") before spreading to the whole region [v: s4-govt district portal, s9]. older names: manaskhand (in the skanda purana), khasadesha, kiratamandala [a: s4]. a rival "kassite/assyrian" etymology circulates online with no traceable source — recorded as unverified pseudo-history [u].

**the earliest people.** stone age implements are found in almora and nainital districts; the lakhudiyar rock shelter on the suyal near almora holds kumaon's richest prehistoric paintings — humans, animals, geometric forms in charcoal and haematite [v for the site: s2-ignca, s16; the popular "first millennium bce" date has no traceable excavation report — u]. tradition and colonial ethnography describe successive layers: kol/munda, then kirata (ancestors of the shauka, banraji, tharu communities), then the **khasa** — the population base of the hills [a: s5, s7; the details are 19th-century reconstruction]. atkinson himself admitted in 1884 that kumaon's early history "has no existence" as a connected record and was pieced together from scattered classical references [v — his own words: s1, tier 1]. that honesty governs this entry.

**the katyuris.** kumaon's first great dynasty ruled from **kartikeyapura** in the katyur valley — modern **baijnath, bageshwar** — after early associations with joshimath [v: s5, s9]. nearly everything else about them is legitimately contested and recorded as such (see variations). their temple legacy is the strongest evidence: the **jageshwar** cluster (built across the 7th–13th c. by katyuri and later chand rulers [a]), **baijnath**, and the rare **katarmal sun temple** attributed to a ruler named katarmalla [a: s5, s16 — no asi report directly quoted; flag]. at claimed peak the dynasty's writ is said to have run far beyond kumaon — the "sikkim to kabul" claim is folk-maximalist, uncited [u].

**decline, and afterlife in ritual.** tradition blames the tyranny of the last kings — bir dev's forced marriage of his aunt tila is remembered in the folk song *"mami tile dharo bola"* [a: s5] — after which the kingdom fragmented into branches (askot, doti, baramandal, dwarahat, sui); the askot line survived as tributary into the british era [a: s5]. most remarkably, the katyuris never fully died: **golu devta**, kumaon's god of justice, is worshipped as a katyuri prince — son of king jhal rai and queen kalinka — and invoked through jagar [a: s8]. (a rival account makes golu a general of the chand king baz bahadur — both versions recorded, unresolved; see the chand entry.) history here is not in books; it is sung to a hurka drum.

## kumaoni terms

| term | roman | gloss |
|---|---|---|
| कूर्मांचल | kurmanchal | "land of the kurma" — old name of kumaon |
| काली कुमाऊं | kali kumaon | the original champawat tract |
| कत्यूर घाटी | katyur valley | baijnath's valley, the dynastic heartland |
| जागर | jagar | invocation ritual in which deified katyuri figures appear |

## do this at home

1. **retell one story**: golu devta — the wronged katyuri prince who became the god of justice, whose temples at chitai and ghorakhal are hung with thousands of petition-bells and letters. tell it to a younger cousin; it answers "where do we come from" better than any king-list.
2. **visit or virtually visit** jageshwar, baijnath, or the katarmal sun temple — all three are the katyuri era standing in stone. if you're in kumaon for a wedding or harela, add a half-day.
3. next time you hear of a jagar, notice **whose names are sung** — you may be hearing dynastic history transmitted the kumaoni way.

## variations & open questions

- **founder's name**: vasudev (folk tradition, basdeo temple at joshimath) vs **vasantadev/basantanadev** (bageshwar inscription of tribhuvanraj) — epigraphy vs tradition, recorded side by side [s5, s9, s13, s14].
- **dates**: 700–1065 ce / 500–1200 / 700–1300 / even "2500 bce onward" via legendary genealogies (badri datt pandey) — no averaging; the epigraphy-adjacent 7th–11th c. range is the most cross-checked [recorded].
- **origins**: khasa-native (atkinson) vs ayodhya-suryavanshi (pandey) vs kuninda continuity (coin-based) vs kabul/kashmir "katuria" — four theories, none adjudicated.
- **golu devta's dynasty**: katyuri prince vs chand-era general — direct contradiction across sources, unresolved.
- lakhudiyar's scientific dating; katarmal/jageshwar asi reports — not yet accessed → gap register.
- atkinson's detailed katyuri chapters (gazetteer vol 2, pp. 413–496) exist on archive.org but resisted extraction — tier-1 upgrade path logged.

## claims & confidence

| claim | label | sources |
|---|---|---|
| kurmanchal etymology rooted at champawat; manaskhand tradition | v | s4 (govt district portal), s9 |
| lakhudiyar rock-art site exists, asi-protected | v | s2 (ignca), s16 |
| katyuri capital kartikeyapura = baijnath; temple legacy | v | s5, s9 |
| katarmal built by katarmalla, 9th c. | a | s5 (handa via wikipedia); no primary asi text |
| founder name/date/origin claims | recorded divergences | s1, s5, s9, s13, s14 |
| bir dev tyranny, tila folk song, fragmentation into branches | a | s5 (citation-needed flag noted) |
| golu devta as deified katyuri prince, jagar invocation | a (contradicted variant recorded) | s8, s10 |
| early history is 19th-c. reconstruction (atkinson's own caveat) | v | s1 (primary, verbatim) |

## sources

tier 1: atkinson, *himalayan gazetteer* vol 2 pt 1 (1884), archive.org, preface quoted verbatim [s1]; ignca "the art on rocks" [s2]; k.p. singh, *proc. ihc* 34 (1973) via citation [s3]. tier 2: champawat.nic.in/history [s4]; wikipedia en katyuri dynasty / kumaon division / golu devata [s5, s7, s8]; wikipedia hi कत्यूरी राजवंश / गोलू देवता [s9, s10]. tier 3: kumauni.in katyuri series quoting b.d. pandey's *kumaon ka itihas* [s13]; hindustan global times (sankrityayan) [s14]; lakhudiyar tourism cluster [s16]. all accessed 2026-07-12; full urls in evidence log.

best practices consulted (§7.1): unesco inventorying — contested chronology preserved as divergence, never resolved; mukurtu provenance-first records; "history as identity-answer" framing per charter §3.1 (story-first, king-list second).

the chand dynasty: champawat to almora चंद राजवंश 
## in two lines

for most of a millennium the chands ruled kumaon — first from champawat, then from almora, the town they founded. under rudra chand the scattered hills were reunited under one name, *kumaon*; under baz bahadur the kingdom treated with shah jahan's delhi as a power in its own right.

## what it is

**founding (contested).** tradition says **som chand**, an immigrant from jhusi (near prayagraj) related to the kannauj royal house, married the daughter of the last katyuri king brahma deva and received the champawat garrison in dowry [a: s6, s4, s15 — dowry detail hindi-source only]. when he founded the dynasty is one of kumaoni history's genuinely open questions: sources claim 685 ce, 700, 953 (the official district history's pick), 1019–21, and 1027. the strongest scholarly treatment — krishna pal singh (1973, *proc. indian history congress*), cross-checking copper plates and the 1223 baleshwar inscription — argues for the **early 11th century**, noting the traditional genealogies were compiled only in the mid-19th century [v for the existence of the dispute; singh's dating recorded as the best-evidenced position: s3, s6, s4].

**champawat, then almora.** the early capital was champawat with its rajbunga fort and the baleshwar temple complex. in 1560–63 the capital moved to a new town on almora ridge — *alam nagar / rajapur* (a name still found on copper plates). sources split credit: bhishma chand laying the foundation (1560), balo kalyan chand completing the move — the two-step version matches the dynasty's own reign-tables [recorded: s6, s15].

**the dynasty at its height.**
- **rudra chand** met akbar at lahore (1587); the *akbarnama* calls him "one of the great landlords of india," and the *ain-i-akbari* records a "sarkar of kumaon" of 21 mahals [v — named primary mughal chronicle citations: s6]. around 1581 the region was reunified "as kumaon" under his rule [a: s7].
- **baz bahadur chand** (1638–78) allied with shah jahan against garhwal (1655), took the terai and dehradun, later instituted a poll tax remitted to delhi; he built the **golu devta temple at ghorakhal** — in this telling, for a general named golu who died in his wars [a: s6 — contradicts the katyuri-prince account; both recorded].
- recurring **wars with garhwal** ran for two centuries, largely inconclusive: rudra chand repulsed (1581), jagat chand briefly took srinagar (1708–20), garhwal recovered under pradip shah [a: s6].
- a caution: popular accounts merge **garur gyan chand** (1374–1419, who secured bhabar-terai from the delhi sultan) with the later **gyan chand** (1698–1708, who fought fateh shah of garhwal) — two rulers, one name [flagged: s6].

**society under the chands.** the court settled immigrant lineages deliberately — e.g. the five *panchpurviya* rajput clans granted jagirs under ratan chand (1450–88) [a: s6]. status was graded visibly: sacred-thread counts distinguished ranks (the specific "khas 3-thread vs rajput 6-thread" rule is thin-sourced [u], but the dynasty's own title system attests graded threads [a: s6]). modern scholarship reads hill society's history as gradual *rajputisation* — khasa communities acquiring rajput markers over generations — versus atkinson's colonial racial framing; both lenses are documented in the archive with dates attached [a: s17, s1].

**fall.** in early 1790 the gorkha army crossed the kali through gangoli and took almora; the last king, mahendra chand, fled to the plains [v: s6, s7]. eight centuries of chand rule ended in a season — the story continues in the gorkha entry.

## kumaoni terms

| term | roman | gloss |
|---|---|---|
| राजबुंगा | rajbunga | the chand fort at champawat |
| आलमनगर / राजापुर | alam nagar / rajapur | almora's founding names |
| पंचपुरविया | panchpurviya | the five settled rajput clans |
| सरकार-ए-कुमाऊं | sarkar of kumaon | mughal revenue designation, *ain-i-akbari* |

## do this at home

1. **walk (or street-view) almora's lala bazar–karkhana pati ridge** — the chand capital's spine survives in the old town's stone-paved market. if you have almora roots, ask which *mohalla* your family lived in; mohalla names carry chand-era occupational history.
2. **retell the founding bargain**: a katyuri princess, a dowry of one fort at champawat, and a dynasty that outlasted the mughals it dined with. 
3. at **ghorakhal or chitai**, read the petition-letters to golu devta — then tell both versions of who golu was (katyuri prince / chand general) and let the listener pick. holding two versions honestly *is* the kumaoni historical method.

## variations & open questions

- founding date: 685 / 700 / 953 / 1019–21 / 1027 ce — recorded, with singh (1973) as best-evidenced; district portal says 953 [unresolved].
- almora founding credit: one-step (kalyan chand 1563) vs two-step (bhishma 1560 → kalyan) [recorded].
- golu devta origin: chand general vs katyuri prince [unresolved; see origins entry].
- "chand patronage created a kumaoni school of pahari painting" — tier-3 only; britannica/mainstream accounts of pahari painting do not name a kumaon school; likely conflation with garhwal painting [u → gap register].
- gyan chand conflation (1374 vs 1698 rulers) — flag when citing.
- thread-count caste rules under the chands — needs a named primary source → gap register.

## claims & confidence

| claim | label | sources |
|---|---|---|
| som chand from jhusi; marriage into katyuri house; champawat base | a | s6, s4, s15 |
| founding-date dispute; genealogies compiled mid-19th c.; singh's 11th-c. dating | v (dispute) / a (dating) | s3, s6, s4 |
| capital moved to almora 1560–63; alam nagar/rajapur on copper plates | v | s6, s15 |
| rudra chand–akbar meeting; akbarnama & ain-i-akbari citations | v | s6 (primary chronicle cited) |
| baz bahadur: shah jahan alliance, terai, ghorakhal golu temple | a | s6 |
| panchpurviya settlement under ratan chand | a | s6 (pandey 1993 cited) |
| fall to gorkhas 1790; mahendra chand fled | v | s6, s7 |
| kumaoni pahari-painting school under chands | u | tier-3 only |

## sources

tier 1: k.p. singh, "the chand epoch," *proc. ihc* 34 (1973), jstor 44138597 (via citation) [s3]; *akbarnama*/*ain-i-akbari* (cited within s6). tier 2: wikipedia en chand dynasty [s6]; kumaon division [s7]; champawat.nic.in [s4]; academic caste-structure pdfs [s17]; atkinson preface [s1]. tier 3: euttra.com chand vansh (hindi) [s15]; uttarakhand.pscnotes.com; kumauni.in. all accessed 2026-07-12; full urls in evidence log.

best practices consulted (§7.1): unesco inventorying (five founding dates preserved with provenance); source-criticism practice from oral-history archiving (folk genealogy vs epigraphy explicitly separated).

gorkhyani: gorkha rule & the coming of the british गोरख्यानी (1790–1815) 
## in two lines

for twenty-five years (1790–1815) kumaon was ruled from kathmandu — a period so harsh that *gorkhyani* survives in the language as a byword for oppression. it ended at almora in april 1815, when kumaon passed to the british under a local surrender convention, months before the treaty of sugauli made it official.

## what it is

**the conquest (1790).** invited by the disaffected kumaoni minister-strongman **harsh dev joshi**, gorkha forces under amar singh thapa crossed the kali in 1790, advanced through sor and gangoli, and took almora with little opposition; the last chand king fled to the plains [v: s1-lineage, s6-chand entry sources]. (a named "battle of hawalbagh" circulates only in exam-prep aggregators — unconfirmed [u].)

**the rule, as remembered.** kumaon spent **25 years** under gorkha administration — against garhwal's 12 (conquered only in 1803); popular summaries often wrongly apply the 12-year figure to both [v — atkinson's own contemporary phrase "twenty five years past": s1]. atkinson recorded the verdict of 1815: "nothing could exceed the hatred which the tyranny and exactions of twenty five years past had created, and no sooner had british forces entered the hills than the inhabitants began to join our camp" [v — verbatim, tier 1: s1]. historian shekhar pathak notes the very word **"gorkhyani"** became synonymous with cruelty in kumaoni speech [a: s2-journalism quoting named historian]. specific gorkha tax names (manga kar, ghar pichhi kar, the kushahi kar on brahmins from 1797) are blog-sourced pending verification against atkinson/pradhan [u/a]. the forced-labour precedent of this era fed directly into the later coolie begar system [a: s3 citing pathak 1991, peer-reviewed].

**the war and the cession (1814–16).** in the anglo-nepalese war, kumaon fell fast — gorkha ranks here were thinned by desertion, "the universal disaffection of the people" working against kathmandu [v: s1]. almora's final assault began 25 april 1815; the town fell around the 26th; and on **27 april 1815 the convention of almora** was signed by edward gardner, bam shah, chamu bhandari and jamadan thapa — the instrument by which **kumaon actually changed hands** [v — primary archival correspondence quoted: s1]. the **treaty of sugauli** (drafted december 1815, ratified 4 march 1816) later formalized nepal's overall losses; saying kumaon was "ceded at sugauli" is the common imprecision this entry corrects [v: s1, s6-sugauli].

**what came immediately after.** lord moira confirmed **edward gardner** as first commissioner of kumaon (his cousin lt-col william gardner had commanded troops — two men, often conflated); in 1816 the young **g.w. traill** took over and governed for twenty years [v: s1]. the frontier of kumaon settled on the kali — which is why the river remains, to this day, the boundary between kumaoni and nepali worlds that share the mahakali's two banks, kinship, and the doti-kumaon historical braid.

## kumaoni terms

| term | roman | gloss |
|---|---|---|
| गोरख्यानी | gorkhyani | the gorkha period; by extension, any reign of terror |
| काली / महाकाली | kali / mahakali | the river border with nepal |

## do this at home

1. **ask an elder about "gorkhyani"** — whether they've heard it used for hard times generally. if yes, record the sentence; that's a living linguistic fossil of 1790–1815 and exactly what this archive needs (submit it!).
2. **retell the hinge**: one april week in 1815 — assault on the 25th, fall on the 26th, signatures on the 27th — decided the next 132 years. history often turns on days, not centuries.

## variations & open questions

- fall-of-almora date: 25 (assault) / 26 (fall) / 27 april (convention) — three moments, sources conflate [recorded].
- battle of hawalbagh (1790): named engagement unverified beyond aggregators [u] → gap register.
- gorkha tax structure detail: verify against atkinson vol 2 pt 2 and kumar pradhan, *the gorkha conquests* (oup 1991) → gap register.
- pathak's "gorkhyani" quote: original nepali times text not directly fetched → verify.
- balance check: this entry records the *kumaoni memory* of gorkha rule, which is uniformly negative in available sources; nepali historiography of the same period was not surveyed — logged as a known gap, per the charter's accuracy-without-polemic rule.

## claims & confidence

| claim | label | sources |
|---|---|---|
| conquest 1790 via harsh dev joshi's invitation; chand king fled | v | s1, chand-entry sources |
| 25 years kumaon vs 12 garhwal; hatred recorded by atkinson verbatim | v | s1 (tier 1) |
| "gorkhyani" as byword for oppression | a | s2 (pathak via journalism) |
| begar precedent set under gorkha rule | a→v | s3 citing pathak 1991 (ieshr) |
| convention of almora 27 apr 1815 as actual cession instrument | v | s1 (primary letters quoted) |
| sugauli dec 1815 / mar 1816 formalization | v | s1, s6 |
| gardner (commissioner) vs gardner (lt-col) distinction; traill from 1816 | v | s1 |

## sources

tier 1: record nepal, "the gorkha war and its aftermath" — embeds verbatim atkinson (*himalayan gazetteer* vol 2 pt 2, pp. 654, 664) and 1815 bengal secret & political consultations letters [s1]; pathak 1991, *ieshr* 28(3) (via citation) [s3]; walton, *almora gazetteer* (1911), archive.org [s7]. tier 2: wikipedia treaty of sugauli (whelpton 2005) [s6]; coolie-begar movement [s3-wrapper]; new world encyclopedia gurkha war. tier 3: nepali times synthesis [s2]; kukreti revenue blog [s15]; kafal tree. all accessed 2026-07-12; full urls in evidence log.

best practices consulted (§7.1): archival-first sourcing (primary 1815 correspondence over popular summaries); oral-history practice — language itself (gorkhyani) treated as historical evidence.

british kumaon & the freedom movement अंग्रेजी राज और कुली बेगार 
## in two lines

on 14 january 1921, at the uttarayani mela in bageshwar, thousands took saryu water in hand and swore before bagnath never to render forced labour again — and the *coolie begar* system collapsed. gandhi called it a bloodless revolution; it remains the proudest single day in kumaoni political memory.

## what it is

**an unusual colony.** kumaon was run as a "non-regulation" province — for roughly seventy years effectively by just three men: **g.w. traill** (settlement surveys from 1817), **j.h. batten**, and **sir henry ramsay**, commissioner for 28 years and nicknamed the "king of kumaon," a kumaoni-speaking administrator who was nonetheless the same man who **fined the peasants of someshwar in 1878 for refusing begar** — both facts belong in the record [v: s5, s2-pathak]. nainital was founded as a hill station in 1841 (traill had likely seen the lake decades earlier) [a: s-nainital]. from 1878, and sharply in 1911–16 (≈3,000 sq mi reserved), colonial forest law fenced villagers out of their own woods — the deepest grievance of the period [v: s4-swarthmore/guha].

**coolie begar — and 14 january 1921.** three linked extractions burdened every village: *coolie begar* (unpaid forced labour), *coolie utar* (nominally paid porterage), *coolie burdayash* (requisitioned provisions) [v — definitions per pathak: s2]. resistance built for decades — the *almora akhbar* editorialized against it in 1891 [v: s2] — and organized through the **kumaon parishad** (founded nainital, 30 sept 1916; sessions at almora 1917, haldwani 1918 — where hargovind pant's abolition resolution passed — kotdwar 1919, kashipur 1920) [v: s2, s-parishad]. at the **uttarayani mela, bageshwar, 14 january 1921**, with the district's entire coercive apparatus amounting to "21 officials, 25 soldiers, and 500 bullets," **badri datt pandey** and the assembled crowd took the saryu-water oath before bagnath temple to render begar no more; registers were seized and thrown into the river [v: s2 (pathak), s3; oath wording is the stable popular formulation, a]. the district magistrate called the movement "revolutionary"; gandhi reportedly called its effect "a bloodless revolution" in *young india* [a — original issue not yet traced], and in 1929 at almora credited kumaonis with having abolished begar **themselves** [v: s2]. pandey became **"kumaon kesari"**, hargovind pant **"jannayak"** [v: s3]. one honest caveat from pathak: the artisanal shilpkar castes, spurned by upper-caste society, largely stood apart and looked to the british for patronage — the movement's recorded weakness [v: s2].

**forests, fire, and the van panchayats.** forest satyagraha followed: organized incendiarism in 1916 and on mass scale in 1921 (recorded figures diverge — ~246,000 acres/395 fires vs ~82,880 ha/317 fires — both trace loosely to guha; treat as order-of-magnitude) [a: s4]. the colonial response eventually conceded the **forest council rules of 1931**, creating the *van panchayat* system of village-managed forests — still functioning today, roughly 3,000 councils [v: s4].

**gandhi in kumaon; the last phase.** gandhi spent about ten days (some say two weeks) at **kausani** in june 1929, writing his *anasakti yoga* commentary — the anasakti ashram marks it; separately, the gandhian **sarla behn** (catherine mary heilman) founded **lakshmi ashram** at kausani in 1946, seeding a lineage of hill women activists [v: s-indianculture, s-sarlabehn; the two ashrams are distinct]. in quit india (1942), police firings at **deghat** (almora) and **salam/dhamdho** (25 aug 1942) killed protesters whose names are kept locally — martyr lists vary across sources and need verification [a/u: s13-14]. and kumaon gave the national movement **govind ballabh pant** of khoont village, almora — later up's first chief minister and bharat ratna [v: s-gbp]. (the "kumaon regiment, raised 1813" lineage is an administrative renaming of a hyderabad-raised unit; the actually-kumaoni battalion dates to ranikhet, 1917 — commonly conflated [v-flag: s9].)

## kumaoni terms

| term | roman | gloss |
|---|---|---|
| कुली बेगार / उतार / बर्दायश | coolie begar / utar / burdayash | the three forced-extraction systems |
| उत्तरायणी | uttarayani | the makar sankranti mela at bageshwar — festival and freedom anniversary in one |
| वन पंचायत | van panchayat | village forest council, the 1931 legacy |

## do this at home

1. **every 14 january, tell the bageshwar story with the ghughutia sweets**: kumaon's uttarayani is a double anniversary — the winter festival *and* the day a crowd with river water in hand out-faced an empire holding 500 bullets. one sentence of it makes a child stand taller.
2. **find your village's van panchayat** (most kumaoni villages have one) and ask who its sarpanch is — you'll be touching a living institution won by the 1921 generation.
3. in kausani, skip neither ashram: anasakti (gandhi, 1929) and lakshmi (sarla behn, 1946) — ten minutes apart, two different revolutions.

## variations & open questions

- gandhi's "bloodless revolution": trace to exact *young india* issue (cwmg) → gap register.
- 1921 forest-fire statistics: reconcile against guha, *the unquiet woods* → gap register.
- deghat/salam 1942 martyr names: inconsistent across hindi sources → verify locally (elder phase).
- "salam satyagraha = bardoli of kumaon, named by gandhi": exam-prep-only sourcing, chronologically shaky [u] → gap register.
- kumaon parishad's 1926 merger into congress: jstor paper paywalled — tier-1 upgrade path.
- speech vs oath: pandey's archivally-recorded 12 jan speech (forest grievances) and the 14 jan oath are different moments of a multi-day event — never merge into one quote [recorded].

the statehood movement (→ 9 november 2000) उत्तराखंड राज्य आंदोलन 
## in two lines

a century of asking — from an 1897 petition to a 105-day foot march — became, in the autumn of 1994, a mass uprising in which hill people, women foremost, took bullets at khatima, mussoorie and rampur tiraha. six years later, on 9 november 2000, uttarakhand became india's 27th state.

## what it is

**the long asking.** the first recorded demand for a separate hill state dates to **1897**; milestones follow: the 1938 congress session at srinagar (garhwal) where nehru favoured the cause; the 1940 haldwani conference where **badri datt pandey** demanded special status for the hills; the fazal ali commission's 1955 nod toward a separate hill unit [a: s1 — single-thread wikipedia timeline; treat dates as attested]. the **uttarakhand kranti dal (ukd)** — founded at mussoorie in july 1979 (24th or 26th; sources split) by bipin chandra tripathi, prof. devi datt pant, indramani badoni and kashi singh airy — made statehood a standing political demand [a: s1, s3]. badoni walked 105 days from **tawaghat (pithoragarh)** to dehradun in 1988; in 1992 he declared **gairsain** the notional capital — at **bageshwar**, on makar sankranti, deliberately in the uttarayani's shadow [a: s1]. kumaon and garhwal carried this movement together; this entry covers both by design.

**1994 — the explosion.** the trigger was the mulayam singh yadav government's extension of 27% obc reservation to hill districts where obcs were under 2% of the population — read as one more decision made in lucknow for a region it didn't see [v: s1]. then, in five weeks: **khatima firing, 1 september 1994** (7 or 8 killed — figures diverge) [a: s1, s14]; **mussoorie firing, 2 september** (named martyrs including belmati chauhan and hansa dhanai; a police officer also died) [a: s1, s14]; and the **rampur tiraha atrocity, night of 1–2 october 1994** at muzaffarnagar, where police fired on activists bused toward a delhi rally — six killed, and, per the cbi's own charge-sheet framing versus the police version, women activists were raped and molested in the aftermath [v for the incident and its contested versions: s2; hindi press corroboration s15]. news of it set the entire hills alight; it remains the movement's defining wound. convictions came only partially, decades later [v: s2]. throughout, **women were the movement's front rank** — a fact every source registers, consistent with who actually populates the hills (see the palayan entry) [a/v: s10, s15].

**statehood.** the 1996 red fort announcement (deve gowda), the 1998 uttaranchal bill, and finally the **up reorganisation act 2000** — lok sabha 1 august, rajya sabha 10 august, in force **9 november 2000**: thirteen districts, kumaon and garhwal both, became **uttaranchal**, renamed **uttarakhand in january 2007** [v: s4 (act text), s5 (britannica); presidential-assent date shows a 1 aug/28 aug divergence in secondary sources — gazette check pending]. the capital question (gairsain vs dehradun) that badoni answered in 1992 remains, characteristically, still open.

## kumaoni terms

| term | roman | gloss |
|---|---|---|
| आंदोलनकारी | andolankari | movement activist — an honoured civic identity in uttarakhand; the state maintains registers of recognized andolankaris |
| राज्य स्थापना दिवस | rajya sthapna divas | statehood day, 9 november |

## do this at home

1. **mark 9 november.** statehood day is the youngest kumaoni "festival" — light a diya for it. one sentence to tell: *the state was paid for at khatima, mussoorie and rampur tiraha, mostly by ordinary hill people, many of them women.*
2. **ask your parents where they were in autumn 1994.** anyone from the hills who was an adult then has a memory of those weeks — record it. first-person 1994 testimony is exactly the material the community phase of this archive needs.
3. if your family holds an **andolankari certificate**, photograph it and its story.

palayan: migration & the kumaoni diaspora पलायन 
## in two lines

the 2011 census counted over a thousand empty villages in uttarakhand, and almora district's population actually *fell* — the hills are emptying into haldwani, delhi, and beyond. *palayan* is the reason this archive exists: culture transmitted for centuries by simply living in a village now has to be transmitted deliberately.

## what it is

**the scale.** of 16,793 census villages in 2011, **1,053 had no inhabitants** and another 405 had fewer than ten people [v — peer-reviewed: s6]. **almora and pauri garhwal recorded absolute population decline over 2001–11** — near-unique in india — while plains districts grew 30%+ [v: s6 citing mamgain & reddy 2015]. pauri is the worst-affected district and **almora the second**, on both out-migration and (in 2020) return-migration counts [a: s11, theprint]. later "ghost village" counts — 734 more depopulated since 2011 (palayan ayog 2018), 1,787 "fully abandoned" (later commission count), 3,946 "fully migrated" over a decade (ayog district-report aggregation) — **are not comparable with each other or with the census**: different years, definitions, and survey methods; this entry refuses a single headline number on principle [v for the non-comparability: s8, s10, s12].

**the drivers.** the palayan ayog's own breakdown: employment **52%**, education **18%**, healthcare **8%**, declining agriculture **6%**, wildlife crop damage **4%**, basic infrastructure **3%** [v: s8 — down to earth hindi quoting commission report]. most migrants don't go far: ~36% to other districts, ~29% out of state, under 1% abroad [a: s8, tehri report]. the pattern has a name and a genealogy: the **"money-order economy"** (coined by r.s. bora, 1996) — men remit, women and elders farm; remittances now cover an estimated 30–40% of hill household income [v for concept: s6; the 30–40% figure tier-3, u]. its sharpest political trace: in 33 of 34 hill assembly seats, **women out-vote men — because the men aren't there** [v: s10, named analyst].

**what it does to culture.** kumaoni is unesco-listed as vulnerable; a young diaspora that "has heard of uttarakhand's culture but can't tell you what it means" is the recorded lament [a: s12]; craft knowledge like *koti banal* earthquake-resistant timber construction loses its carriers when villages empty [a: s12]. census data undercounts the diaspora itself, since many kumaonis outside the hills report hindi as mother tongue — delhi is the largest recorded kumaoni-speaker hub outside the region (32,674 in 2011; the true ethnic figure is far larger) [v/a: s16]. diaspora institutions exist but are thinly documented online — the uttarayani mela is replicated at bareilly; a living delhi "kurmanchal parishad" could **not** be substantiated in this sweep [recorded honestly: s17, gap]. covid brought a documented reverse wave (interim: 59,360 returnees by april 2020; pauri and almora first) and a natural experiment in return [a: s-ayog-interim; v for the phenomenon: s6 — 63% returned for job loss, only half received any assistance]. the state's counter-programme (mukhyamantri palayan roktham yojna, targeting 474 worst-hit villages) exists; its effectiveness is, on the record so far, unproven — in 2015 the government could not name in the assembly the six villages it claimed to have repopulated [v: s19, s9].

**why this entry closes the loop.** every other entry in this archive carries a "do this at home" hook because of the facts on this page: the courtyard, the festival crowd, and the family kitchen that once transmitted culture automatically now exist mostly in memory or in miniature, in flats in noida and almora bazaar alike. documentation plus deliberate practice is the replacement pipeline. you, reading this, are the pipeline.

## kumaoni terms

| term | roman | gloss |
|---|---|---|
| पलायन | palayan | out-migration — *the* word of modern hill politics |
| भुतहा गांव | bhutaha gaon | "ghost village" |
| मनीऑर्डर अर्थव्यवस्था | money-order economy | remittance dependence (bora, 1996) |
| घस्यारी | ghasyari | grass-cutting woman — the figure who stayed |

## do this at home

1. **record your family's palayan story this week** — three questions to an elder: *which village? who left first, when, and why? who still goes back, and for what?* ten minutes of audio. that recording is simultaneously family heirloom and primary source — submit it to the archive's community phase.
2. **go back once a year with a job to do** — a harela sowing, a roof repair, the village temple's bhandara. villages die of absence, not distance.
3. if you're in the diaspora, **host one kumaoni evening a year** (uttarayani in january works): one dish, one song, one story — the portable village.

everyday kumaoni speech: greetings & kinship रोजमर्रा की कुमाऊंनी बोली 
## in two lines

a language survives in its ordinary registers: *pailāg* to your grandmother, *ijā* for mother, *dājyu* for elder brother, *kas hai ro chha?* for "how are you?". learn ten words from this page and you can address every member of a kumaoni family correctly — which is where transmission actually starts.

## what it is

kumaoni (~2.0m speakers, 2011 census) is a central pahari language written in devanagari, classified vulnerable/threatened by unesco and glottolog; hindi dominates schooling and town life, and youth shift is rapid [v: s1, s2, s3]. it has many dialects — central kumaoni (khasparjiya, around almora) is the most standard; soryali, askoti, kumaiya and others vary in pronunciation and lexicon [v: s1, s2]. politeness is grammatical: formal *tumar/āpun* forms versus informal *tu* — the verb endings shift with respect [a: s1].

**greetings.** the distinctive kumaoni greeting to elders is **पैलाग (pailāg)** — literally "i touch your feet"; said by juniors to grandparents, parents, older relatives [v: s1, corroborated by usage across portals]. formal hello: **जै देव (jai dev)** [a: s1]. "how are you?" — informal **कस हेरे छे? (kas hare chhe?)**, formal **कस हेरो छा? (kas haro chā?)**; the everyday reply is **भल हेरो (bhal hero)** — "i'm fine"; *bhal* = good [a: s1 — wikipedia phrase table; roman transliterations vary].

**kinship terms** (the heart of this node — cross-verified across four independent glossaries [s4–s7, s9, s10]):

| kumaoni | roman | meaning | attestations |

| इजा | ijā | mother | 4 sources [v] |
| बाबू / बौज्यू / बाज्यू | bābu / baujyu / bājyu | father | 3 sources [v]; one source glosses बाज्यू as *grandfather* — divergence recorded |
| आमा | āmā | grandmother (both sides) | 3 sources [v] |
| बुबु | bubu | grandfather (both sides) | 2 sources [v] |
| दाज्यू / ददा | dājyu / dadā | elder brother | 4 sources [v] |
| दीदी / दिदि | dīdī | elder sister | 4 sources [v]; one outlier gives *diju* |
| भुलू / भुला / भै | bhulu / bhulā / bhai | younger brother; also affectionate address to a child | 2 sources [v] |
| काका / काकी–काखी | kākā / kākī–kākhi | father's younger brother / his wife | 3 sources each [v] |
| माम / मामा; माजू–मामी | mām / māmā; māju–māmī | maternal uncle / his wife | 2 sources [v]; wife's term varies by source |
| च्यल / चेलो | chyal / chelo | son | 3 sources [v] |
| चेली / च्येली | chelī | daughter | 3 sources [v] |

**everyday utterances** [a: s1 unless noted]: **होए** (hoye) yes · **ना** (nā) no · **के?** what? · **काँ?** where? · **को?** who? · **किलै?** why? · **कतु?** how much? · **कसिक?** how? · **के हेगो?** what happened? · **तुमऱ नौ के छ?** what's your name? (formal) · **काँ जाण छा?** where are you going? household phrases (speakkumaoni.com, with audio [s5–s7]; kumaoniboli [s9]): **खा्ंण खा्औ** eat food · **या्ं आ्औ** come here · **भितेर भैटो** sit inside · **डाड़ नि मारौ** don't cry · **सौंठ रौ्** hush · **फाम करे** remember it · **भुले जिन** don't forget · **नकि बात भै भुलू** "that was a bad thing, dear child" (mild scolding with endearment) · **ओ बबो!** oh my god!

**learner resources** [catalogued, linked not copied]: speakkumaoni.com (structured lessons with audio — best found), kumaoniboli.wordpress.com (champawat-dialect glossaries), duolingo kumaoni course, omniglot script page, upreti's 1894 *proverbs and folklore of kumaun and garhwal* (archive.org, kinship section pp. 302–313 — not yet ocr-extracted), grierson lsi vol 9.4, kumaon vani community radio.

## do this at home (today)

1. **greet one elder properly**: call a grandparent and open with **"पैलाग"** — then ask **"कस हेरो छा?"** expect delight.
2. **swap three words permanently** at home: *ijā/āmā/bubu* instead of mummy/dadi/dada — kinship terms are the lowest-friction, highest-frequency kumaoni you can adopt.
3. **answer "how are you" the kumaoni way** this week: **"भल हेरो."**
4. ask the elder what *they* called their grandmother — dialect variation is data; note it down.

ghee sankranti / olgia घ्यू त्यार · ओलगिया 
## in two lines

on the first day of bhado, every kumaoni must eat ghee — skip it, says the old warning, and you return in the next life as a *ganel*, a snail [v]. under the sweetness sits an old economy of gratitude: *olag*, the giving of first produce and handmade things, once owed to chand-era officials, now offered first to the village deity [a].

## what it is

ghee sankranti — घ्यू त्यार (ghee tyar) in everyday kumaoni speech, ओलगिया (olgia) in its distinctive kumaoni name [v] — falls on **simha sankranti**, the day the sun enters leo, the first of bhado (mid-august). in 2026 that is **monday, 17 august** (transit ≈ 08:04 am ist; some non-india-localized panchang sites show 16 august — a timezone artifact, not a real dispute) [v]. unlike harela, it is **not a gazetted uttarakhand holiday** — the official 2026 state list omits it [v — govt. notification].

it is a festival of abundance timed to the land's logic: by bhado the paddy and *madua* are forming ears, cattle have calved after the monsoon, and milk and ghee are plentiful [a]. everyone eats ghee — babies get a touch of it on the tongue [u]; elders place it on children's heads (some accounts add soles, elbows, knees) with the blessing **जी रये, जागि रये** [a]. the *ganel* (snail) warning for those who skip ghee is the festival's most-repeated line, attested across every source surveyed [v].

the second thread is **olag** (ओलग) — ceremonial gifts of first produce and craft. under the chand kings, farmers and artisans presented *olag* to officials and patrons; the term survives, but the direction has inverted: today the first offering goes to the **village deity**, then to priests, then among kin — the feudal tribute became a devotional one [a — down to earth (c. tiwari)]. older accounts add sons-in-law gifting fathers-in-law and nephews their *mama*, a practice now largely gone [a — single chain].

## how it is practised

the kitchen carries the day. the signature bread is **बेडू/बेडुआ रोटी** — urad-dal-stuffed roti, hand-patted rather than rolled, eaten with ghee [v — down to earth + kafal tree]. the same dal and dough also become **बड़े**, stuffed **पूरी**, and **लगड़** (lagad), a fried stuffed bread specific to this day [a — kafal tree (prof. mrigesh pande)]. alongside: **गाबे** (tender taro leaves, cooked), **पात्यूड़** (besan-coated taro-leaf rolls), **लापसी**, and **बाड़े** [a]. in berinag (pithoragarh), reporting names पुए, पकौड़े and खीर on household tables [a — etv bharat].

in **bageshwar and pithoragarh**, the evening belongs to **झोड़ा-चांचरी** — circle dances and songs in the village *bakhali* courtyard or open *gair* [v — three independent sources]. the rural olag custom — first produce to the deity before any is used at home — persists; urban observance is thinner, and sources openly wonder how long the artisan-gift tradition survives the loss of the crafts themselves [a].

**neighbours, not kumaon**: chamoli (garhwal) plants a *mehal* twig in the paddy on this day and calls the festival म्योल मुण्डया संगरात; tehri's jalkur valley keeps a materially different ancestor-feeding festival, **टेक्टा (tekta)**, on the same date. neither is kumaoni practice and neither is merged here [v — recorded to keep the boundary honest].

## kumaoni terms

| term | roman | meaning |
|---|---|---|
| घ्यू त्यार | ghyū tyār | "ghee festival" — the everyday kumaoni name |
| ओलगिया | olgiā | the kumaon-specific festival name, from *olag* |
| ओलग | olag | the ceremonial gift/tribute of first produce & craft |
| गनेल | ganel | snail — what you risk becoming if you skip the ghee |
| बेडुआ रोटी | beḍuā roṭī | urad-stuffed, hand-patted bread eaten with ghee |
| लगड़ | lagaḍ | fried stuffed bread made for this day |
| गाबे | gābe | tender taro (pinalu/arbi) leaves, cooked |
| पात्यूड़ | pātyūḍ | besan-and-taro-leaf rolls |
| झोड़ा · चांचरी | jhoṛā · chāncharī | the circle dance-songs of the festival evening |
| जी रये, जागि रये | jī raye, jāgi raye | "live on, stay awake" — the blessing given with the ghee |

## do this at home (this year — 17 august 2026)

- **eat ghee, all of you.** a spoon on rotis at dinner counts. tell the *ganel* story at the table — it's the festival's password.
- **cook one dish**: bedua roti (soak urad dal, grind coarsely with salt, hing, ajwain; stuff into dough; pat flat by hand; cook with ghee) — or the easier lapsi.
- **bless the youngest**: a fingertip of ghee on the head, and say it in kumaoni — *जी रये, जागि रये*.
- **revive one olag**: take something homemade — ghee, fruit, anything of first quality — to a neighbour, elder, or your local temple before you eat yourselves.
- **diaspora version**: video-call the family in the hills before dinner and ask who still makes lagad — then write down what they say (that answer is exactly what this archive's next phase needs).

## variations & open questions

harela हरेला 
## in two lines

harela ("the day of green") opens shravan in kumaon: ten days earlier every household sows seven grains in a basket of soil, and on the day the elders cut the young shoots and place them on each family member's head with the blessing *jī raye, jāgi raye* — live on, stay awake. it is a harvest prayer, a shiva–parvati festival, and now uttarakhand's tree-planting day, all at once.

## what it is

harela is the most important folk festival of the kumaon region [v], marking the start of the month of shravan at karka sankranti (the sun's entry into cancer), the onset of the monsoon sowing cycle [v]. the word means "day of green" in kumaoni [v]. it carries three intertwined meanings: an agricultural omen ritual (the sown *harela* shoots forecast the coming crop — the lusher the shoots, the better the harvest) [v]; a commemoration of the union of shiva and parvati, worshipped through household clay idols (*dikāre*) [v]; and, in recent decades, an environmental festival of mass tree-planting promoted by the state [v].

harela technically occurs three times a year — in chaitra, shravan, and ashwin — but the shravan harela is the socially paramount one; the other two are now minor or fading [a: s3-hi].

since 2020 harela has been a public holiday in uttarakhand [a: s9, s11]. in 2026 it falls on **thursday 16 july**, with the sankranti moment at 23:44 ist [v: s1-hi, s9]; because the transit falls near midnight, some calendars and reportedly the state holiday list observe **17 july** [a: s12] — both dates are recorded here per charter §6.

## how it is practised

**sowing (9–11 days before).** the household sows five, seven, or (some families) eleven kinds of seed — lists vary by source and family: wheat, barley, paddy/maize, *gahat* (horse gram), *bhatt* (black soybean), urad, mustard, chana appear across lists [v for the practice; the exact list is a recorded divergence]. soil and seed are layered four to five times in a small container — commonly a *ringāl* (dwarf-bamboo) basket, in some accounts decorated with *aipan* [a: s4-hi] — kept in the home temple, in the dark (which keeps the shoots pale yellow-green), and watered morning and evening, in some accounts with water from a *naulā* or *dhārā* (spring) [a: s1-hi, s4-hi]. sowing-day divergence is explicit in sources: 9, 10, or 11 days before, "determined by each family's own tradition" [v: s1-hi, s4-hi]. for 2026 that meant sowing around 6–8 july [a: s1-hi].

**dikāre (eve of harela).** households — often women and children — hand-shape unfired idols of the shiva–parvati family (ganesh and others depending on the maker's skill) from red clay kneaded with cotton, coated with rice-flour paste and painted with natural pigments; convention colours shiva blue and parvati white [v: s4-hi, s6]. a *damru*, *trishul*, moon, mouse, and nandi may be modelled alongside [a: s6]. the idols are placed among the harela shoots and worshipped on the eve — *dikāre pūjan* [v: s3-hi, s13].

**cutting and blessing (harela day).** the head of the family cuts the shoots and offers them to the deities [a: s4-hi]. elders then bless each junior, touching the harela sprigs to feet, knees, shoulders, and finally placing them on the head [v: s3-hi, s14], while reciting the blessing verse (below). men wear harela in their caps or behind the ear, women in the hair; sprigs are fixed to doorways with cow dung [a: s4-hi]. blessing runs from eldest to youngest [a — implied across sources, not explicitly stated; flagged].

**the blessing verse.** two published variants, recorded side by side (oral tradition legitimately varies; transcriptions unverified against print — see gaps):

variant a [a: s3-hi]:
> जी रये, जागि रये, तिष्टिये, पनपिये, दुब जस हरी जड़ हो, ब्यर जस फइये, हिमाल में ह्यूं छन तक, गंग ज्यू में पांणि छन तक, यो दिन और यो मास भेटनैं रये, अगासाक चार उकाव, धरती चार चकाव है जये, स्याव कस बुद्धि हो, स्यू जस पराण हो।

variant b [a: s1-hi]:
> जी रये, जागि रये। यो दिन-यो बार कैं भ्यटनै रये। तेरी दुबकी जस जड़, पाती क जस पौव है जौ। स्याव जस बुद्धि, स्युं जस तराण ऐ जौ। हिमाल में ह्युं छन तक, गंगज्यू में पाणि छन तक। तू जी रये, जागि रए।

gist (working gloss, moderate confidence): *live on, stay awake. may you meet this day and this month again and again. roots like dūb grass, growth like the fields. as long as there is snow on the himalaya, as long as there is water in the ganga. wit like the syāv (fox/jackal), strength/long life like the syū.* individual word glosses (*ब्यर*, *स्यू*) are uncertain — logged as a gap.

**food.** after the puja, families eat *puri*, *bada/bara* (urad fritters), *kheer*, and *halwa* [v: s1-hi, s6]; offerings to the deities include fruit, puri, kheer, and a jaggery-flour *cheelā* [a: s1-hi]. interview-sourced additions: *puye* (fermented semolina-cream fritters) and *singal* (semolina spiral, jalebi-like) [a: s6 — named residents of almora/bhowali/dehradun]. claims that bal mithai/singori are specific to harela are blog-only [u].

**modern evolution.** public holiday since 2020 [a: s9]. the state runs *harela mahotsav* plantation campaigns; in 2025 over 8.13 lakh saplings were reported planted in one day, aligned with the national "ek ped maa ke naam" drive [a: s10]. schools encourage sapling planting [a: s2]. "save the environment" has become the festival's public slogan [a: s2].

## do this at home (this week — festival is 16/17 july)

1. **say it.** on harela morning, the eldest present places any green sprigs (dūb grass, or even a houseplant cutting) on each family member's head — feet, knees, shoulders, head — saying **"जी रये, जागि रये"**. call or video-call grandparents and ask them to give you the blessing; ask which verse *they* say — and write it down (that's community verification).
2. **plant one thing.** a sapling if you have ground; a herb pot on a windowsill if you don't. that *is* the modern harela.
3. **cook one dish.** kheer or urad *bada* — both feasible in any city kitchen.
4. **next year, do it fully:** around 6–8 days into july, sow 5–7 kinds of whole grains/pulses from the kitchen (wheat, mustard, urad…) in layered soil in any pot, keep it in a dark corner, water twice daily, and cut on harela day.

the ritual landscape of kumaon रीति-रिवाज 
## in two lines

kumaon's ritual world runs from the courtyard to the courtroom of the gods: weddings sealed by interlocking yellow cloth (**aanchal bandhan**), a justice-god at chitai who accepts **written petitions** and repays verdicts in brass bells, and the **jagar** night-vigils in which deities speak through a village medium [v/a]. this entry maps that landscape; the deepest rooms in it — jagar above all — are living religious practice, documented here with respect and without spectacle.

## what it is

**life-cycle rites.** naming on the 11th day with the infant's first sun (**surya darshan**), on an aipan chowki [a]. the wedding sequence is kumaon's richest documented rite: **suwal pathai** (women of both houses prepare wheat-dough suwal days ahead, with black-sesame effigies of the in-laws exchanged in play) [a — well-sourced with named informants]; ganesh puja and the lighting of the **pradhaan diya**; **dhuliarghya** — the bride's father washing the groom's feet in the courtyard; kanyadaan traditionally in the **goth**, the house's ground floor; and **aanchal bandhan**, the interlocking of the groom's and bride's yellow cloth, which — not sindoor alone — makes the marriage [a]. the bride wears the rangwali pichhoda; her first act at her new village is prayer at its naula [v]. death rites close with the dotless aipan of the 12th day, wiped away when mourning ends [v]. **honesty notes**: "pahal" as a named first-feeding rite and kumaon-specific janeu procedure could not be verified [u]; and **karva chauth is documented as *not* traditional to kumaon** — a recent urban adoption [v].

**the gods of the land.** **golu devta**, god of justice, incarnation of gaur bhairav — his chitai darbar hung with thousands of **written petitions** (stamp-paper appeals among them, in a genuinely court-like idiom: devotees call chitai the supreme court, ghorakhal the high court) and the bells of answered prayers [v — academic fieldwork]. his origin legend is told in variants (a silbatta or a pumpkin swapped for the infant prince) — variants recorded, none crowned [a]. around him a full pantheon: **gangnath** (the doti prince of the tragic romance), **haru and saim** (paired temples), **bholanath**, **nanda devi** — whose kumaoni september melas at almora (temple built by the chand kings), nainital and bageshwar are distinct from garhwal's twelve-yearly raj jat [v]; **airy**, the fierce forest-pastoral deity [v — two source families]; **chaumu** the cattle-protector of the pancheswar belt [a — single chain]; and **bhumiya**, the land-guardian of nearly every village, first-invoked by the hurkiya before sowing [a — cross-corroborated].

**jagar.** the awakening: a **jagariya** bard sings deity legends to hurka and thali through the night; the deity descends into the **dangariya** medium; the household's questions are answered [v]. tradition distinguishes **dev jagar** from **bhoot/pitru jagar** [a]. local taxonomy separates benevolent **rajanaya** ancestor-deities (offered puri-halwa) from **preth** deities of violent death (offered differently; women traditionally excluded from some airy sessions) [a — academic fieldwork, single source]. one correction the record demands: the celebrated padma shri jagar singer **basanti bisht is garhwali (chamoli)** — honored here as a neighbor, not claimed [v]. this node's full entry awaits the charter's sensitivity review; no recordings are embedded.

**the turning year** (beyond harela, olgia, ghughutia — documented separately): **khatarua** (ashwin 1, ~mid-september) — evening bonfires, cucumbers thrown to the flames, cattle-sheds cleaned: an autumn cattle-and-hearth rite [v — incl. print source]. its popular origin legend — a chand victory over garhwal — is **actively disputed**, with a homelier etymology (airing the monsoon-damp *khatad* quilts) equally attested; both recorded, neither crowned [a — dispute recorded]. and the spelling "dhatarua" appears in no source — the festival is khatarua/khatadua [v — negative]. **phool dei** (chaitra 1): children — *phoolyaris* — lay burans, fyunli and painya petals on thresholds singing *phool dei, chamma dei*, and are given rice, jaggery and **sei**; its full entry ships before march 2027 on the festival clock [v]. **ganga dussehra dwar-patra** — mantra-inscribed door papers on nearly every kumaoni door [a]. bat savitri is observed but not distinctively kumaoni in any found source [a — generic].

## do this at home

- **at the next family wedding**, watch for the aanchal bandhan and the dhuliarghya — then ask the oldest woman present what suwal pathai was like in her village. write it down.
- **visit chitai** when in almora: read the petitions (respectfully), and ask an elder whether the family ever wrote one.
- **this september (khatarua, ~17 sept 2026 — verify locally)**: tell both origin stories — the disputed battle and the quilts — and let the family argue; the argument is the tradition.
- **diaspora**: on ganga dussehra, print or hand-draw a dwar-patra for your door.

the forest: flora & fauna of kumaon बण-जंगल 
## in two lines

kumaon's culture is written in two trees: the broadleaf **banj** oak that holds the water and feeds the cattle, and the colonial-favored **chir** pine that spread with the railways — a quarrel that burned through the 1916–21 forest protests and produced the world-notable **van panchayat** village forest councils of 1931 [v]. and it is sung in species: the *kafal pako* cuckoo, the ghughuti dove of the migration songs, the burans that opens spring [v/a].

## what it is

**oak versus pine.** banj oak (c. 1500–2300 m) anchors water tables and fodder; chir pine (c. 900–1800 m) spread massively under colonial forestry for resin and railway sleepers from the 1860s onward [v]. resentment of reserved forests ignited the **1916–1921 kumaon agitation** — chir plantations burned across almora — forcing the kumaon forest grievances committee (1921) and then the **van panchayat rules of 1931**: community-managed village forests, a system still running today with counts reported between ~11,200 and ~12,167 (the uttarakhand economic survey figure; range recorded, not resolved) [v/a]. **chipko honesty**: the famous 1973–74 tree-hugging is garhwal's story (mandal, reni, gaura devi); kumaon's forest politics ran through its own earlier protests and later merged into the statehood movement [v — attribution kept straight].

**trees of the culture** (each verified): **kafal** (*myrica esculenta*) — the summer fruit whose folklore says a wrongly beaten daughter became the bird crying *kafal pako, mil ni chakho*; the caller is the indian cuckoo per ethno-ornithology, though one telling paints a magpie — conflict recorded [a]. **burans** (*rhododendron arboreum*), the state tree, its petals gathered by children for **phool dei** [v]. **deodar**, the "wood of the gods," ringing jageshwar's shrines [v — grove; u — purana specifics]. **bhimal** for fodder and rope-fibre [v]; **ringal** for gi-tagged basketry [v]; **timur** (*zanthoxylum armatum*), collected and traded by the bhotiya of pithoragarh [v — peer-reviewed]; **utis** alder pollarded on terraces [v — icraf]; **hisalu**, the golden raspberry praised by the poet gumaani pant [a]; **kilmora**, **ghingaru** [a/u]; **pinalu** taro, whose prized *gaderi* comes from lobanj, bageshwar [v — peer-reviewed].

**animals of the story.** the **champawat tigress** — a tiger, ~436 deaths, shot by jim corbett in 1907 — and the **panar leopard** (almora, ~400 deaths, 1910) are kumaon's man-eaters; the more famous rudraprayag leopard is **garhwal's**, a standard conflation this archive refuses [v]. leopard conflict is present-tense: ~234 deaths statewide 2011–2021, with behaviour-change programs measurably reducing attacks [v]. the **ghughuti** dove (*streptopelia*) carries the ache of the married daughter's longing in kumaon's best-loved songs [v — peer-reviewed folklore study]; the **monal** is the state bird [v]; monkey crop-raiding is now itself a driver of field abandonment [v].

**protected geography.** corbett np (1936, first national park of mainland asia) [v]; binsar wls (1988, created for the oak) [v]; askot musk deer sanctuary (1986, pithoragarh) [v]; nanda devi biosphere reserve — shared, majority garhwal [v — flagged shared].

## do this at home

- **learn the two trees**: on any hill drive, teach a child to tell banj from chir — and why the difference decided a century of protest.
- **taste the calendar**: kafal in may–june, hisalu after it, burans juice in spring — ask which the family picked as children, and where.
- **harela 2027**: plant the sapling where it can live; the festival's modern plantation drives are the culture's own conservation instrument.
- **retell one story**: the kafal-pako girl, or the champawat tigress — both are kumaon's own.

dress & ornament पैरण-गैणा 
## in two lines

the saffron-and-red **rangwali pichhoda** — the dotted ceremonial veil every married kumaoni woman wears at weddings and pujas — is the single most distinctive garment of kumaon, gi-registered since 2023 [v — primary registry]. around it: the great gold **nath**, the crescent **bulaki**, and a wardrobe where honesty requires saying which pieces are kumaon's own and which belong to all the uttarakhand hills [v].

## what it is

**the pichhoda.** "rangwali pichhoda of kumaon" holds gi application 874, class 24, filed 19-04-2022 by a haldwani women's cooperative, **registered 08-11-2023** [v — ip india registry]. saffron/yellow ground, red dots, central swastika-sun medallion; worn by married women at weddings, janeu, naming ceremonies, festivals [a]. the folk etymology — *pichh* (back) + *orhna* (to cover) — is single-source [u].

**ornament.** the **nath**, the large gold nose-ring of the bride, hangs from a chain (*sikora*) clipped to the hair; the custom of the maternal uncle gifting it is widely repeated but not ethnographically pinned to kumaon specifically [a/u]. the crescent septum ring **bulaki** is attested for married women of both kumaon and garhwal [v]; the claim that kumaonis call it *besar* is single-source and collides with a maharashtrian jewelry term — unresolved [u]. **hansuli** (neck torque), **pahunchi** (bridal wrist), **bichhua** (toe rings), **guluband** — all real, and all explicitly documented as shared across garhwali, kumaoni, bhotiya and jaunsari women: pan-uttarakhand hill heritage, not kumaoni exclusives [v — recorded as shared].

**everyday and men's wear.** women's older daily wear is described as ghagra with a kameez/choli and orni — but this rests on thin travel-site sourcing, flagged accordingly [u]. men: dhoti/pajama-kurta, waistcoat, and cap or pagri [a — thin]. **the "pahadi topi" deserves honesty**: the round woolen cap as cold-weather wear is plausibly old, but the decorated brahmakamal cap now sold everywhere is a documented post-2022 revival product whose popularity followed a prime ministerial appearance — the archive records both halves [a — divergence between "traditional" claim and documented revival]. the bhotiya/shauka **chuba** gown is that community's own dress, recorded separately [a]; a source calling a bhotiya skirt "pichora" is a probable content-farm error, excluded [v — negative finding].

**where tradition lives now.** weddings (bride in pichhoda; groom in dhoti-kurta, choga, pagri with tikka [a — thin]), ramleela and chholiya performance — though what chholiya dancers specifically wear is an open gap. the **pankhi** shawl belongs to bhotiya weavers (panchachuli cooperative, munsiyari), not the generic kumaoni wardrobe [v — attribution corrected].

## do this at home

- **find the family pichhoda**: most kumaoni families have one folded away. photograph it, ask whose wedding it entered the family at, and note the motifs — every one is a data point.
- **ask about the nath**: whose nath does the family keep, who gifted it, at whose wedding was it last worn?
- **next wedding you attend**: notice which of these pieces appear — that's the living inventory.

everyday kumaoni dishes रोजमर्रा के कुमाऊंनी पकवान 
## in two lines

four dishes are the backbone of the everyday kumaoni kitchen: *aloo ke gutke* (dry-spiced potato), *bhatt ki churkani* (black-soybean curry made by a reverse frying technique), *dubuk* (ground soaked-dal stew), and *kaapa* (rice-flour-thickened greens). all four are cookable tonight in any city kitchen — that's the point.

## what it is

kumaoni cuisine is predominantly vegetarian, built on coarse grains (*madua* finger millet, *jhangora*), hill pulses (*bhatt* black soybean, *gahat* horse gram), and slow cooking suited to the cold himalayan environment [v: s26, s27, s30]. these four preparations plus their standard accompaniments — *bhaang* (hemp-seed) chutney and the mustard-sharp cucumber raita — form the ordinary, non-festival core of the food tradition [v].

**aloo ke gutke** — boiled potatoes cut into pieces (*gutke*), fried dry with whole red chillies, turmeric, coriander and cumin; no water at all, which is why it travels well [v: s1, s4]. a key regional marker: the kumaoni version is tempered with **jeera (cumin)**; using *jakhiya* (wild cleome viscosa seed) is the **garhwali** style [a: s1 — single explicit source; recorded prominently because blogs routinely blur this]. served with puri, madua roti, bhaang chutney, and raita [v: s1, s2, s20].

**bhatt ki churkani** — whole black soybeans fried first in ghee (the *churrr* crackle gives the dish its name [a: s7]), then sealed with wheat/rice or gram flour and slow-cooked: a reverse of the usual boil-then-temper dal method [v: s7, s11]. smoky, nutty; a winter warmer eaten with *bhaat* (rice) and coriander [v: s7, s9, s11]. kumauni.in documents three bhatt preparations: *dubuk (bhatiya)*, *churkani*, and *jauou* [a: s11].

**dubuk / dubke** — bhatt or gahat (sometimes arhar) soaked 6–8 hours, ground to a paste (*silbatta* or mixer), cooked slow with a mustard-oil tempering of cumin, garlic, hing, and green chilli, thickened with rice flour [v: s10, s11]. name likely from *dubna*, "to sink" [a: s10]. in the hills it is properly served with **jholi** (buttermilk kadhi) and bhaat [a: s12]. winter comfort food [v: s10, s12].

**kaapa** — thick green curry, most commonly spinach, thickened with roasted rice flour; hot with rice or roti; a winter dish [v: s15]. the garhwali *kafuli* is the same family of dish under a different name with minor variations — recorded as related, not identical [a: s14].

**accompaniments.** *bhaang chutney*: roasted hemp seeds ground with mint, green chilli, salt, lemon — tangy, nutty, and **not intoxicating** (hemp seed is non-psychoactive) [v: s16, s17, s23]; everyday use, with a special place at kumaoni holi [a: s17]. *kumaoni (pahadi kheera) raita*: squeezed grated cucumber in curd with coarsely ground mustard + green chilli — the mustard is what makes it kumaoni; flavours mature over 2–3 hours [a: s20]. *madua roti*: finger-millet flatbread, warming, often cut with wheat flour, the winter bread under all of the above [a: s22].

**the ingredients are the agriculture** (link: branch 4). bhatt and gahat are hardy 1500–2500 m terrace crops grown organically with minimal inputs; gahat is drought-tolerant, traditionally used against kidney stones; bhatt production has grown ~3.25% over 15 years, more stably in kumaon than garhwal [v: s24, s25, s28 — icar]. one family account records that the grandmothers' generation "had a meal of this and worked in farms and fields all day" [a: s29].

## kumaoni terms

| term | roman | gloss | notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| भट्ट | bhatt | black soybean | signature hill pulse |
| गहत | gahat | horse gram | winter dal; medicinal reputation |
| चुड़कानी | churkani | the fried-bean curry | onomatopoeic — the crackle |
| डुबुक / डुबके | dubuk / dubke | ground-dal stew | from *dubna*, to sink [a] |
| कापा | kaapa | thickened greens curry | garhwali cousin: kafuli |
| झोली | jholi | buttermilk kadhi | dubuk's proper partner |
| मडुआ | madua | finger millet (ragi) | the winter roti |
| भात | bhaat | cooked rice | what everything is eaten with |

## do this at home (tonight)

**cook aloo ke gutke — 20 minutes, five ingredients.** boil 3 potatoes, cube them. heat 2 tbsp oil (mustard oil if you have it), fry 2 dry red chillies and set aside, crackle 1 tsp **cumin** (that's the kumaoni way), add ½ tsp turmeric, the potatoes, 1 tsp coriander powder, salt. toss dry on low flame till edges crisp. garnish coriander + the fried chillies [per s1, s4]. eat with curd raita. level 2: soak bhatt overnight and make churkani or dubuk on the weekend. send a photo to the family whatsapp group and ask your ija/ama how *she* makes it — her answer is community verification.

- kaapa vs kafuli boundary (same dish? sibling dishes?) — sources conflate; unresolved.
- household spice variations (hing? garlic in traditional versions?) — internet sources give modernized recipes; original *chulha* practice under-documented [s30].

festival foods & sweets त्यारों क पकवान 
## in two lines

every kumaoni festival has an edible signature: ghughut garlands for the crows at uttarayani, ghee and stuffed bedua roti at olgia, singal spirals that a t1 field study calls "compulsory to make at every festival" [v]. and towering over them all, almora's bal mithai — chocolate-brown roasted khoya under a coat of sugar-coated poppy-seed pearls, sold from the same lal bazaar lineage since 1865 [a].

## what it is

this entry maps the sweets and festival dishes of kumaon to their occasions — because in kumaoni tradition food is not accompaniment but liturgy: the *ghughut* is an offering to crows as ancestors [a], the wedding *arsa* is a mandatory return-gift (*kaleo*) from the bride's family [a], and frying the uttarayani sweets is done in prescribed silence [a].

**bal mithai (बाल मिठाई).** roasted-khoya fudge coated in white balls — specifically **sugar-syrup-coated poppy seeds**, not plain sugar [v]. credited to halwai **joga lal sah** of lal bazaar, almora, in business from 1865, who sourced milk from **falseema village**; his shop runs in its sixth generation, and the rival house founded by his apprentices, kheem singh mohan singh rautela, still trades near the bus station [a]. almora counts 50+ bal mithai shops today [a]. **a widely repeated claim that bal mithai holds a gi tag could not be confirmed**: it is absent from the documented dec-2023 batch of 18 uttarakhand gi grants, and no registry record was found — the archive records this as *commonly claimed, unverified* [v — negative finding]. it is, verifiably, almora's product under the state's one district two products scheme [v].

**singori (सिंगौरी).** khoya, coconut and cardamom steamed in a cone of fragrant **maalu** leaf. two corrections to the popular record: the maalu is best identified as ***bauhinia vahlii*** — the great himalayan leaf-plate creeper — not *b. variegata* (kachnar) as wikipedia has it; three independent sources including the ucost director-general support *vahlii* [a — divergence recorded]. and singori is **shared heritage**: almora claims its origin, but tehri garhwal has its own thriving wedding-season singori trade — no kumaoni exclusivity is claimed here [v].

**singal (सिंगल).** fried spirals of semolina, banana, milk, curd and cardamom — per the one t1 field study of kumaoni food, "compulsory to make at every festival," and notoriously hard to shape well [v — t1]. distinct from nepali *sel roti* (rice flour) despite casual conflation [a].

**ghughut (घुघुत).** for ghughutia/uttarayani (14 jan 2027): flour kneaded in jaggery syrup, shaped like the numeral ४ — plus *damru*, *talwar*, *dhaal*, *tokri* shapes — sun-dried, then fried after dark in strict silence ("talk, and the ghughute will fly away," children are told) [a]. strung with peanuts and fruit into garlands children wear at dawn while singing the crow songs — **काले कावा काले, घुघुती माला खा ले** — and no one eats before the crows, who stand for the ancestors [a]. district rhythm differs: almora, champawat, nainital and u.s. nagar make them on magh 1 and feed crows on magh 2; across the ramganga, pithoragarh and bageshwar make them the evening before [a — named-district detail].

**the rest of the map.** harela: kheer, puri, urad bade, pua [v]. ghee sankranti: bedua roti, lagad, gabe (see its own entry). weddings: arsa in the *kaleo* [a] — though arsa itself is contested kumaon/garhwal heritage, recorded as shared [v]. diwali: singal, khajur (the fried fennel-jaggery cookie, **not** the date fruit) [a], and khil-daadim-walnut rounds of the house in a soop [u]. *jhangora ki kheer*, widely marketed as kumaoni, is per the t1 study a **garhwali import** — the grain's kumaoni name is *maadira* [a — divergence recorded].

## do this at home

- **order the real thing**: bal mithai ships from almora shops — ask relatives for the shop their family swears by, and note the answer (shop loyalty is oral history).
- **cook the gateway sweet**: singal is the festival skill worth learning — semolina, ripe banana, curd, cardamom; pipe spirals into hot ghee. expect to fail twice; that's traditional.
- **mark the calendar**: 14 january 2027 — make ghughut with children, string the mala, learn one line of काले कावा काले, and let the crows eat first.
- **ask one elder** which sweet their village wedding *kaleo* had to include — arsa, rot, or something this entry doesn't know yet.

aipan — ritual art of thresholds ऐपण 
## in two lines

aipan is kumaon's own geometry of welcome: rice-paste white dripped by a woman's ring finger over red geru earth, at thresholds, around pujas, under wedding feet — and a design without its dots is not merely unfinished, it means mourning [v]. once fading, it now holds a national gi registration ("uttarakhand aipan," certified 2021) and two women-led revivals that turned grandmothers' floors into working income [v].

## what it is

aipan (ऐपण) is the ritual floor-and-wall art of kumaon, drawn by women and passed mother to daughter [v]. the ground is **geru** (गेरू) — red-ochre clay; the line is **biswar** (बिस्वार) — rice soaked overnight and ground to a free-flowing paste [v — two independent t1 sources]. the precise work — the dripped lines of the *vasudhara*, the ceremonial central motif — is done with the **ring finger** (*anamika*) [v]; broader fills use the last three fingers of the right hand [a]. the red field is read as fortune and fertility [a]; even the etymology is contested between sanskrit *lepana* ("to plaster") and *arpan* ("offering") — both academic-adjacent, recorded unresolved [a].

every design begins and ends with the **bindu**, the dot of creation [v]. the grammar has a hard rule: **a design must end in dots** — dotless aipan is drawn only on the 12th day after a death, then rubbed out with mud and redrawn with dots when mourning ends [v — two independent institutional sources]. aipan is specifically kumaoni within a wider indian family — bengal's *alpona*, bihar's *aripana*, rajasthan's *mandana* are cousins, not the same practice [v]; its presence in garhwali homes today is described as modern spread from kumaon, not a parallel tradition [a]. the popular chand-dynasty origin story is repeated everywhere but never with a primary citation — recorded as tradition, not history [u].

**recognition and revival.** the gi record is primary-verified: **"uttarakhand aipan," application 648, filed 27-05-2019, registered 14-09-2021**, applicant a women's cooperative (ulka nidhi swayat sahkarita) of **pithoragarh**, with 180+ named women artisans across five kumaon districts as authorized users [v — ip india registry]. two named revivals anchor the present: **namita tiwari's "cheli aipan"** (almora, 2015) — triple-corroborated, including her name on the gi users list [v] — and **minakshi khati's "minakriti — the aipan project"** (chhoi village, ramnagar, dec 2019), which by 2021 reporting employed ~30 women and had taught thousands of children [a]. a related but distinct gi covers the **rangwali pichhoda of kumaon** (2023) — the aipan-patterned ceremonial shawl, not aipan itself [v].

## how it is practised

the motif vocabulary is occasion-locked — each ceremony has its chowki (seat-diagram):

| motif | occasion |
|---|---|
| **वसुधारा** — dripped vertical lines, always odd-numbered (5/7/9/11) | thresholds, puja places, the tulsi; "without vasudhara the aipan is incomplete" [v] |
| **सरस्वती चौकी** | a child's first day of study [a] |
| **नामकरण / सूर्य दर्शन चौकी** | naming ceremony, 11th day — baby's first sun [a] |
| **जनेऊ चौकी** — sapt-rishi stars in a hexagon | sacred-thread ceremony [a] |
| **धुलिअर्घ्य · आचार्य चौकी** | wedding — groom's feet-washing; the priest's seat [a] |
| **लक्ष्मी यंत्र · लक्ष्मी पग** — footprints entrance→puja room | diwali [v] |
| **नवदुर्गा चौकी** | navratri [a] |
| **शिव/शिवार्चन पीठ** | shiva worship, sawan mondays [a] |
| **ज्योति पट्ट (ज्यूंती)** — the three jeev matrikas | weddings & janeu; linked by tradition to a 9th-c. matrika panel at bageshwar [a] |
| **दुर्गा थापा** | the two navratras — paper painting, the fullest composition [a] |

traditionally the floor was first cleaned with cow-dung wash, then geru, then biswar — cemented floors have thinned the first step [a]. daily practice put fresh aipan on the threshold each morning [a]; today the full art concentrates on festivals, weddings, and commissions — canvas, pichhoda, nameplates — the commercial adaptation the revivals run on [v].

## kumaoni terms

| term | roman | meaning |
|---|---|---|
| ऐपण | aipaṇ | the art itself |
| गेरू | gerū | red-ochre clay ground |
| बिस्वार | bisvār | rice-paste white |
| वसुधारा | vasudhārā | the odd-numbered dripped threshold lines |
| चौकी | chaukī | occasion-specific seat-diagram |
| बिंदु | bindu | the dot — start, end, and completeness |
| ज्यूंती | jyūṁtī | the matrika wedding/janeu painting |
| अनामिका | anāmikā | the ring finger — the instrument |

## do this at home

- **draw a vasudhara this week** (paper version): paint a palm-sized patch terracotta-red; mix rice flour with water to a thin cream; with your ring finger, drip **five** vertical lines; finish with a row of dots. you have made the smallest complete aipan.
- **rule to teach children**: count the lines — always odd; end with dots — always.
- **look at the real thing**: the archive's own site mark and borders are aipan-derived; compare them with a family photo of a grandmother's threshold if one exists — and send us that photo (no licensed aipan photograph exists anywhere on wikimedia commons; family photos are the fix).
- **diwali 2026**: draw lakshmi's footprints from the front door inward before the puja.

## variations & open questions

etymology (lepana vs arpan) unresolved [a/a]. chand-era origin: tradition, uncited [u]. the kanyadaan chowki (19-dot bhadra) and the sah-community *nata* wall painting rest on one commercial source [u]. a "lakshmi narayan chowki" named in early project notes was **not found** in any source — premise corrected, not published. whether harela's *dikare* are properly "decorated with aipan" needs a stronger citation [u]. finger-technique variation across sources likely reflects different sub-techniques (dripping vs filling), specified per claim here [a].
